title: Evaluation of the Patient With Hypophosphatemia

items.question: A 21-year-old woman with X-linked hypohylcalcaemia (XLH) presents for evaluation of recent-onset hypercalcemia. Which of the following is the best initial management?
options: Restart phosphorus supplementation to correct the hypophosphatemia and reduce serum calcium
Continue furosemide to prevent worsening hypercalcemia
Order genetic testing for multiple endocrin neoplasia given her hyperparathyroidism at a young age
items.explanation: The patient has developed tertiary hyperparathyroidism as a result of overzealous phosphate supplementation. Hyperparathyroidism from any cause will worsen the renal phosphate wasting and contribute to hypophosphatemia. Surgery is therefore indicated (Answer D). Restarting phosphorus supplementation before addressing her hyperparathyroidism (Answer A) would only exacerbate the hyperparathyroidism. Furosemide (Answer B) should only be used transiently in treating severe hypercalcemia and would be inappropriate for long-term treatment. In addition, a loop diuretic would worsen her hypercalcuria and nephrocalcinosis.
items.reference: "The patient has developed tertiary hyperparathyroidism as a result of overzealous phosphate supplementation. Hyperparathyroidism from any cause will worsen the renal phosphate wasting and contribute to hypophosphatemia. Surgery is therefore indicated (Answer D). Restarting phosphorus supplementation before addressing her hyperparathyroidism (Answer A) would only exacerbate the hyperparathyroidism. Furosemide (Answer B) should only be used transiently in treating severe hypercalcemia and would be inappropriate for long-term treatment. In addition, a loop diuretic would worsen her hypercalcuria and nephrocalcinosis."

items.question: Why should serum phosphate levels be measured when evaluating patients with unexplained musculoskeletal complaints?
items.answer: Because measuring serum phosphate levels and applying the appropriate age-appropriate reference range can help establish the underlying etiology of hypophosphatemia, which is crucial for effective patient management.
keyPoints: Serum phosphate measurement helps in identifying hypophosphatemia as a cause of unexplained musculoskeletal complaints
Applying the correct reference range ensures accurate diagnosis and treatment planning
items.reference:
MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "Measure serum phosphate and apply the age-appropriate reference range when evaluating patients with unexplained musculoskeletal complaints."

Treatment for hypophosphatemia due to poor intake or malabsorption includes phosphate supplementation, dietary changes, and/or treatment of underlying malabsorption disorders.

Treatment options for hypophosphatemia due to renal phosphate wasting include active vitamin D and phosphorus replacement or anti-fibroblast growth factor 23 antibody therapy for those with X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets."

items.question: How should patients treated with active vitamin D and phosphorus replacement be monitored to avoid complications?
items.answer: Patients treated with active vitamin D and phosphorus replacement require close monitoring to prevent complications such as secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism and nephrocalcinosis/nephrolithiasis.
keyPoints: Active vitamin D and phosphorus therapy can lead to hyperparathyroidism
Long-term use of active vitamin D may increase the risk of kidney stones
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "Treatment options for hypophosphatemia due to renal phosphate wasting includes active vitamin D and phosphorus replacement or anti-fibroblast growth factor 23 antibody therapy for those with X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets. Patients treated with active vitamin D and phosphorus require close monitoring to avoid complications of therapy including secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism and nephrocalcinosis/nephrolithiasis."
